answer:
Three operative passages were found, one per tradition:

1. SRUTI (smritis/Sanskrit): Manusmrti 1.108 (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch.1, p-47b537): 'The rule of conduct is the highest Dharma, declared in both Sruti and Smarta; therefore a self-possessed twice-born should always be devoted to it.' Sanskrit transliteration: 'acarah paramo dharmah srutyuktah smarta eva ca.' Here sruti is named as the Vedic-heard revelation that grounds dharmic conduct; it functions as a juridical-textual category derived from its etymology as 'that which is heard.'

2. WAHY (Iqbal's Urdu): Bang-e-Dara Part 3, ch.4 'Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai' (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch.4, p-644d2a): 'A Voice replied: Your tale is filled with grief, / Your cup of tears o'erflows beyond belief. / Your frenzied cry has reached the heavens' ear; / How bold the tongue of your wild heart, I hear! / Yet you gave grievance such a graceful art, / You taught a slave to speak with God, heart to heart.' Urdu transliteration: 'Aayi aawaaz, gham-angez hai afsaana tera...' The word wahy does not appear verbatim in the transliteration, but the poem's entire structure enacts wahy: a divine Voice (aawaaz) descends from the Throne in response to human lament — the classical Quranic schema of revelation as downward acoustic transmission.

3. REVELATIO (Cynewulf): Elene ch.1 (cynewulf-elene-d2d132, ch.1, p-acdc07 and p-306e06): 'A shining spirit appeared in his sleep, / a heavenly herald, handsome of feature, / who called out his name through the closing darkness... The messenger of glory spoke with majesty, / his voice a balm for the leader's bale-sorrows. / Constantine, king! Cast out your terror... By this sign, conquer.'

WHICH PLACES MOST WEIGHT ON AUDIBILITY: Cynewulf's Elene. The revelation in Elene is constituted by sound at every level: the herald physically calls out Constantine's name in the dark, the voice is named as a balm (its acoustic quality is the vehicle of comfort), and the divine command is spoken aloud ('By this sign, conquer'). The written letters on the Rood are secondary — the spoken word precedes and enables the vision. By contrast, Manusmrti's sruti is etymologically acoustic but operates in the text as a fixed textual-juridical category; and Iqbal's aawaaz-as-wahy emphasizes the content of the divine speech and the human-divine dialogue rather than the sound-quality of the voice itself. Cynewulf alone makes the voice's sensory character — its brightness, its balm-like effect on a fear-stricken man — the operative substance of the revelation.
